matter.title: Grace C. Roe v. Maria Q. Fairbanks (eviction)
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe seeks to evict Maria Q. Fairbanks from 22 Tidewater Lane, Apt 3, Augusta based on nonpayment of rent, having served a 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent on 2026-02-21.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe seeks to evict Maria Q. Fairbanks from 22 Tidewater Lane, Apt 3, Augusta based on nonpayment of rent, having served a 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent on 2026-02-21.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe rents the residential unit at 22 Tidewater Lane, Apt 3 in Augusta, Maine to Maria Q. Fairbanks under an expired lease continuing month-to-month at $1581 per month. The tenant is approximately 5 month(s) behind, owing about $7905.

The landlord served a 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent on 2026-02-21. The notice period has expired and the tenant remains in possession, so the landlord now brings a Forcible Entry and Detainer action seeking a judgment for possession (and, where allowed, unpaid rent).

The case proceeds as summary process in the District Court. The tenant may raise defenses such as breach of the implied warranty of habitability or improper notice, which the court must resolve before issuing a writ of possession.
undisputed_facts: Maria Q. Fairbanks occupies the unit at 22 Tidewater Lane, Apt 3, Augusta.
A 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent was served on 2026-02-21.
disputed_facts: The exact amount of rent owed and any setoff for conditions.
Whether the premises met the implied warranty of habitability.
Whether the notice was proper in form and timing.

third_parties.name: Cobblestone Partners Co.
third_parties.relevance: Manages the building and documented the nonpayment.

third_parties.name: Local code enforcement office
third_parties.relevance: Relevant if the tenant raises a warranty-of-habitability defense.

third_parties.name: Wesley R. Calloway
third_parties.relevance: Can testify to disturbances or occupancy.

third_parties.name: Regional housing authority
third_parties.relevance: Administers the tenant's housing voucher, if any.

exchanges.question: What is the rental arrangement?
exchanges.answer: an expired lease continuing month-to-month at $1581 a month for the unit at 22 Tidewater Lane, Apt 3.

exchanges.question: What is the problem?
exchanges.answer: The tenant is about 5 months behind, owing roughly $7905. The basis is nonpayment of rent.

exchanges.question: What notice did you give?
exchanges.answer: I served a 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent on 2026-02-21, and the period has run.

exchanges.question: Has the tenant complained about conditions?
exchanges.answer: There may be a habitability argument; I want to be ready for it.

exchanges.question: What do you want?
exchanges.answer: Possession of the unit, and the unpaid rent if the court will award it.

goals: Obtain a judgment for possession of the premises.
Recover unpaid rent where the court allows it in the FED action.
Secure a writ of possession if the tenant does not vacate.
priorities: Proper notice and service to avoid dismissal.
desired_outcomes: A judgment for possession and issuance of a writ if needed.
non_starters: A defective notice that forces the case to be refiled.

constraints.budget: Cost-conscious; the landlord wants a prompt, clean summary process.
constraints.timeline: Wants possession before the unit's carrying costs mount further.

issues.issue: Sufficiency of the notice to quit
sub_questions: Was the correct 7-day or 30-day notice used and properly served?
issues.client_position: Contends the notice was proper in form and timing.

issues.issue: Implied warranty of habitability as a defense
Issuance and execution of the writ of possession
sub_questions: When may the writ issue after judgment?

issues.issue: Grounds for forcible entry and detainer
sub_questions: Is there a valid statutory ground for eviction?
Is there any claim of retaliation?

issues.issue: Whether long non-collection of rent waived or modified the rent obligation, undercutting the stated arrears
issues.governing_law: Common-law waiver / estoppel / implied modification (not an FED statute)
sub_questions: Did years of neither charging nor collecting rent signal the parties treated rent as not owed?
If so, is the arrears figure in the 7-day notice overstated, making the notice defective?
issues.notes: [confounder:waiver-by-noncollection; fact-specific / NOT codified — common-law waiver-estoppel that courts often reject]

evidence.title: Notice to quit with proof of service
evidence.description: The served notice that starts the clock.

motions.title: Request for issuance of writ of possession
motions.relief: A writ directing the tenant's removal if the judgment is not satisfied.

affirmative_defenses.defense: Breach of the implied warranty of habitability
affirmative_defenses.basis: Alleged uninhabitable conditions justifying a rent setoff. 14 M.R.S. § 6021.

affirmative_defenses.defense: Waiver / estoppel / modification of the rent obligation
affirmative_defenses.basis: The landlord neither charged nor collected rent for about 27 months. NOTE (overbroad-risk): mere delay or non-collection is NOT enough — waiver, estoppel, accord, or implied modification needs proof of an agreement, intentional relinquishment, or detrimental reliance; the argument frequently fails, and acceptance of rent does NOT auto-waive a Maine 30-day no-cause notice (14 M.R.S. § 6002).

facts.notice_type: 7-day notice to quit for nonpayment of rent
